Alt Afrikaner Investigation

Project Snooker:
How MTN Tried to Trade the Rooivalk to Iran

The 22-year paper trail from a South African boardroom to a $4.2 billion reckoning, the Afrikaner engineering brilliance caught in the middle, and the sitting president's name on the board minutes.

$4.2B
Turkcell Damages Claim
$31.6B
Projected Irancell Revenue
22
Years of Litigation
7,000
Pages of MTN Documents

Denel AH-2 Rooivalk Mk1 — Combat Support Helicopter — General Systems Overview. 16 Squadron, SAAF, AFB Bloemspruit

I. The Weapon

The Helicopter That Shouldn't Exist

In 1984, while most of the world was trying to isolate South Africa into submission, a team of engineers under an Afrikaner government at the Atlas Aircraft Corporation began designing something the country's military desperately needed and no one on earth would sell them: a dedicated attack helicopter.

They called it the Rooivalk. Red Falcon. United Nations Security Council Resolution 418 had locked South Africa out of the global arms market. No Western manufacturer would supply an attack helicopter. The South African Defence Force needed one to counter Soviet-supplied T-55 tanks in the Angolan theatre and escort troop transports into contested airspace.

Atlas engineers, working from a facility outside Pretoria, took the airframe philosophy of the Aérospatiale SA 330 Puma and rebuilt it from the ground up. They designed a tandem-cockpit gunship with a 20mm cannon, anti-tank missile capability, and avionics competitive with the best in the world. The prototype first flew on 11 February 1990.

Dr Paul Potgieter, who led the Rooivalk programme and was later honoured by the South African Air Force (SAAF) as the father of the Rooivalk, went on to found Aerosud, a private aerospace firm built from the same engineering nucleus. Aerosud was formed in 1990 by former Atlas employees, including key designers of both the Rooivalk and the Atlas Cheetah fighter programme. That company became an internationally recognised supplier to Airbus, Boeing, and BAE Systems.

In 1992, Atlas Aircraft was absorbed into the state-owned defence conglomerate Denel (Pty) Ltd. Only twelve production Rooivalks were ordered by the SAAF. But the programme's industrial impact was enormous: it seeded the engineering base that allowed South African firms to participate in the Saab Gripen, the BAE Hawk, the AgustaWestland AW109, and the Airbus A400M.

This was Afrikaner engineering capital. Built under sanction. Proven in combat. Refined over decades. And in 2004, it was allegedly offered to Tehran.

II. The Deal

The Licence, The Fish, and The Boardroom

On 18 February 2004, Turkish telecommunications giant Turkcell won the bid to operate Iran's second private mobile network. The contract was projected to generate $31.6 billion in revenue over fifteen years. MTN Group, Africa's largest mobile operator, came second.

What happened next is documented in a 70-page complaint filed on 28 March 2012 in the United States District Court for the District of Columbia (Case 1:12-cv-00479-RBW), in sworn testimony from former MTN executive Chris Kilowan, and in thousands of pages of internal MTN documents.

According to those filings, MTN did not accept its loss. The company launched what can only be described as a parallel diplomatic operation, leveraging personal relationships with senior ANC government officials to offer Iran three things Turkcell could not: defence cooperation, nuclear diplomacy, and weapons.

"MTN sought to obtain illegally what it could not win through honest competition."
Cedric Soule, counsel for Turkcell, King & Spalding
III. The Shopping List

Codename: "The Fish"

In August 2004, MTN accompanied Defence Minister Mosiuoa "Terror" Lekota on a trip to Iran, which the cellphone company organised and paid for. At meetings with Iranian Defence Minister Ali Shamkhani, they allegedly signed a confidential memorandum of understanding. The promise, according to the US court filing: South Africa would deliver "heaven, earth, and fish".

"The Fish" was the code name for a combination of military cooperation and big-ticket defence equipment. The US District Court filing identifies each item:

🚁
Rooivalk Attack Helicopters
Based on the US Apache platform. Produced by Denel. The most desired item on the list.
Primary Target
📡
Frequency-Hopping Radios
Encrypted military communications systems capable of sending secure messages.
US Components
🎯
Sniper Rifles
South African-manufactured precision weapons systems.
Controlled
💥
G5 Howitzers
South African-developed long-range 155mm projectiles containing US component parts.
US Components
🛡️
Armoured Carriers
Landmine-proof personnel carriers developed in South Africa by Reutech.
Controlled
📡
Coastal Radar Systems
Technology developed by a division of Reutech.
Controlled
🖥️
Pilot HUD Technology
Air pilot display computer chips manufactured in the Western Cape.
US Components
🚀
Missile Development
Access to a missile development company located off the Ivory Coast.
Weapons System
⚠ Key Finding

This equipment was unavailable to Iran through legitimate means because of US and international restrictions. The National Conventional Arms Control Committee (NCACC) had already rejected earlier official attempts to sell the Rooivalk to Iran. MTN's alleged strategy was to use political connections to circumvent that rejection.

IV. The Network

The Players

The MTN board received regular reports on the status of MTN's efforts to displace Turkcell. According to the court filings and investigative journalism, the following individuals occupied central roles:

🇿🇦
MTN Non-Executive Chairman (2001-2013)
Cyril Ramaphosa
Appointed to the MTN Board in 2001, elevated to chairman in 2002. Sat as non-executive chairman throughout the entire period during which the Iranian licence was secured. Reportedly helped facilitate a meeting between Iran's nuclear negotiator Hassan Rouhani and President Mbeki. Now President of South Africa.
ANC President
🇿🇦
MTN Group CEO
Phuthuma Nhleko
Accompanied Minister Lekota to Iran. Wrote to Dr Mahmoudzadeh confirming that "national security and defence matters between South Africa and the Islamic Republic of Iran" had been "fully accepted by MTN." Named defendant in the ongoing lawsuit.
Named Defendant
🇿🇦
MTN Commercial Director, Strategic Projects
Irene Charnley
Allegedly boasted influential connections to Denel. Sent a fax facilitating a meeting between Denel and the Iran Helicopter Support and Renewal Company. Claims to have met with Denel CEO Victor Moche. Named defendant. Denies all allegations.
Named Defendant
🇿🇦
SA Ambassador to Iran
Yusuf "Jo-Jo" Saloojee
Codenamed "Short John." Allegedly paid $200,000 to help deliver pro-Iran votes from South Africa at the IAEA. Showed Kilowan the Iranian arms wish list. Attended meetings with Iranian Defence Minister.
Codename: Short John
🇮🇷
Iran Deputy Foreign Minister
Javid Ghorbanoghli
Codenamed "Long John." Allegedly paid $400,000 to politically undermine Turkcell's position and ensure the licence transfer to MTN.
Codename: Long John
🇿🇦
SA Minister of Defence
Mosiuoa "Terror" Lekota
MTN organised and paid for his trip to Iran in August 2004. Attended meetings with Iran's Defence Minister where the MOU was allegedly signed. Later denied MTN paid for any visit. Now president of COPE.
ANC Cabinet
🇿🇦
Former MTN Executive
Chris Kilowan
Former MTN executive and director on the board of MTN Irancell. Took 7,000 pages of internal MTN documents. Provided sworn testimony detailing the bribery and arms-for-licence scheme. MTN dismisses him as a "disgruntled former employee."
Key Witness
🇮🇷
Director, Sairan (Iran State Arms Co.)
Dr Mahmoudzadeh
Drafted the specific wish list that became "The Fish." Primary Iranian interlocutor for the defence cooperation package. Linked to the Iran Helicopter Support and Renewal Company (IHSRC).
Iran Defence
$4,200,000,000
Turkcell's Damages Claim
Automatically tripled under the US Anti-Terrorism Act if liability is found
V. Chronology

The Paper Trail

🇿🇦 1984
Rooivalk Programme Begins
Atlas Aircraft Corporation begins development of the attack helicopter under arms embargo. Dr Paul Potgieter leads the design team.
🇿🇦 11 February 1990
Rooivalk Prototype First Flight
The Rooivalk flies for the first time. The Border War has already ended. Aerosud is founded the same year by former Atlas engineers.
🇿🇦 1992
Atlas Absorbed into Denel
Atlas Aircraft Corporation becomes part of state-owned Denel. The Rooivalk continues under the Denel Aviation banner.
🇿🇦 2001-2002
Ramaphosa Joins MTN Board
Cyril Ramaphosa appointed to MTN Board in 2001, elevated to non-executive chairman in 2002. Will hold this position until 2013.
🇹🇷 18 February 2004
Turkcell Wins Iranian Licence
Turkcell is awarded the bid for Iran's second mobile network. MTN comes second. The projected revenue: $31.6 billion over 15 years.
🇮🇷 April 2004
Iran Tests MTN's Willingness
Sairan requests MTN arrange meetings with Denel and Defence Minister Lekota. MTN commits to procuring military equipment including eavesdropping technologies.
🇮🇷 July 2004
Charnley Meets Ambassador Saloojee
Charnley allegedly tells Saloojee that MTN has access within the Ministry of Defence to facilitate delivery of military items to Iran.
🇮🇷 August 2004
"Heaven, Earth, and Fish"
MTN accompanies Lekota to Iran. Nhleko, Charnley, Lekota, and Saloojee meet with Iranian Defence Minister Shamkhani. They allegedly promise to deliver "The Fish" in exchange for the licence.
🇿🇦 Late 2004
Rouhani Visits Mbeki
MTN allegedly pays for Iran's chief nuclear negotiator Hassan Rouhani to travel to Cape Town to meet President Mbeki. Facilitated by Charnley, Ramaphosa, and Saloojee.
🇿🇦 16 November 2004
The Denel Fax
Charnley allegedly sends a fax facilitating a meeting between Denel and the Iran Helicopter Support and Renewal Company. Confirms she was meeting with Denel CEO Victor Moche.
🇦🇹 September 2005
South Africa Abstains at IAEA
The IAEA Board votes on Iran's non-compliance with nuclear obligations. South Africa abstains, despite previously supporting similar resolutions. The timing coincides with the licence decision.
🇮🇷 27 November 2005
MTN Secures the Licence
MTN announces it holds 49% of Irancell, formally awarded the mobile licence. Days after South Africa's IAEA abstention.
🇺🇸 28 March 2012
US District Court Filing
Turkcell files 70-page complaint in Washington DC. Case 1:12-cv-00479-RBW. Details "The Fish," bribery allegations, and IAEA vote manipulation.
🇿🇦 April 2025
Supreme Court of Appeal Rules
SCA overturns the High Court's 2022 dismissal. Rules that South African courts have jurisdiction. MTN appeals to the Constitutional Court.
🇺🇸 March 2026
US Congressional Scrutiny
Congresswoman Stefanik writes to BNY Mellon urging investigation of MTN ties. Cites Ramaphosa's chairmanship during the licence period. Five ATA lawsuits filed against MTN in the US.
VI. The Nuclear Dimension

Trading Votes at the IAEA

The weapons were only half the equation. Iran also needed diplomatic cover at the International Atomic Energy Agency, where its nuclear enrichment programme was under intense scrutiny.

According to both the court filings and Kilowan's testimony, MTN learned that Iran valued South Africa's ability to lead non-aligned nations on nuclear votes in international bodies. The company allegedly arranged and financed a trip to South Africa by Iran's chief nuclear negotiator, Hassan Rouhani, so he could meet with President Mbeki.

"We paid for the hotels in Cape Town. We paid for the dinners, everything. We bought presents for them. And we made sure that he got to see President Thabo Mbeki."
Chris Kilowan, sworn testimony, US District Court

In September 2005, South Africa abstained on the IAEA resolution finding Iran in non-compliance. This was a notable departure from its previous voting pattern. The licence was awarded to MTN weeks later. Whether the proximity constitutes causation remains a matter for the courts. But as one analyst observed: in political economy, sequence is a signal.

The financial incentives were explicit. The court filing details how Ambassador Saloojee was allegedly paid $200,000 to deliver pro-Iran IAEA votes, and Iran's deputy foreign minister Ghorbanoghli allegedly received $400,000 to undermine Turkcell's position.

VII. The Chairman

The Ramaphosa Question

Cyril Ramaphosa was appointed to the MTN Board in 2001 and elevated to non-executive chairman in 2002. He held that position until 2013. His chairmanship spans the entire period during which the Iranian licence was pursued, negotiated, and secured.

According to the AmaBhungane Centre for Investigative Journalism and the US court filing, the MTN board of directors, including Ramaphosa, Nhleko, Dabengwa and Charnley, received regular reports on the status of Turkcell's licence and MTN's strategies.

Ramaphosa, alongside Charnley and Saloojee, reportedly helped facilitate the meeting between Iran's nuclear negotiator Rouhani and President Mbeki in Cape Town, using their combined political connections.

Washington Takes Notice

As noted in a March 2026 BusinessTech analysis: "The fact that our current President, Cyril Ramaphosa, served as MTN's Non-Executive Chairman during this pivotal era (2001-2013) has not gone unnoticed in Washington." The US refusal to grant a diplomatic visa to MTN's current chairman Mcebisi Jonas, in his capacity as Special Envoy, confirms that Washington no longer distinguishes between South African corporate interests and state policy.

MTN relied on its longstanding connections with the ANC government. Ramaphosa's political reach meant that, according to one analysis, anybody they needed to reach was a phone call away. These were not passive board members receiving sanitised reports. The court filings allege an integrated strategy in which corporate ambition, diplomatic access, and defence policy were woven into a single commercial objective.

VIII. The Reckoning

Where It Stands

In April 2025, the Supreme Court of Appeal ruled that South African courts have jurisdiction to hear the Turkcell case. MTN applied to the Constitutional Court for leave to appeal. Turkcell filed its opposition on 4 June 2025. As of early 2026, none of the allegations have been tested in court on their merits. MTN maintains they are baseless.

Separately, MTN faces litigation under the US Anti-Terrorism Act from over fifty American plaintiffs who allege that the company's transactions aided Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) activities. Five lawsuits were filed in 2025. Under the ATA, damages are automatically tripled, potentially reaching $5 billion (approximately R80 billion).

Legal observers have described a possible finding of liability as a potential "black swan event" for the Johannesburg Stock Exchange. A guilty verdict could trigger what one economist calls the "USD Death Penalty," severing MTN from the US financial system, with cascading consequences for South Africa's credit rating, its African Growth and Opportunity Act (AGOA) trade benefits, and the broader economy.

The engineers who built the Rooivalk under international isolation never imagined their creation would appear on a coded weapons list offered to a sanctioned Middle Eastern regime by a telecommunications company with ANC heavyweights on its board. That it did tells you something about what happened to South Africa's defence industrial base after 1994, and about the distance between building things and trading them.

IX. The Machine

The Rooivalk

To understand what MTN allegedly offered to trade, you have to understand what the Rooivalk actually is. It is not a modified import. It is not a licensed copy. It is an indigenous combat helicopter designed under arms embargo by South African engineers who were told the world would not sell them one, so they built their own.

The name means Red Kestrel in Afrikaans. Development began in 1984 under the project codename Chickadee, later renamed Impose, at the Atlas Aircraft Corporation, a division of the Armaments Corporation of South Africa (ARMSCOR). The engineering challenge was formidable: design an attack helicopter capable of defeating Soviet-supplied T-55 tanks in the Angolan theatre, using only components that could be manufactured domestically or sourced despite comprehensive UN sanctions.

The Rooivalk in flight with full weapons loadout. Frontal visual signature: just 1.28 metres. Source: Denel

The design philosophy centred on four principles: not to be seen; if seen, not to be hit; if hit, to sustain flight; if flight could not be maintained, the crew must survive the crash. These were not theoretical statements. They were engineering requirements born from real combat losses in southern Angola.

Atlas engineers chose to base the airframe on the Aérospatiale SA 330 Puma's dynamic systems, which the South African Air Force already operated. This gave them commonality with the Atlas Oryx transport helicopter, simplifying logistics and maintenance in the field. But the Rooivalk was no modified Puma. The tandem-cockpit gunship was a ground-up redesign, featuring a stepped crew arrangement to reduce sun glare, armoured stations resistant to 12.7mm armour-piercing rounds, crashworthy seats rated to an 11 m/s sink rate, and self-sealing fuel tanks that could survive direct hits without exploding.

The prototype XDM (Experimental Development Model) was rolled out on 15 January 1990 and first flew on 11 February 1990. It was the first attack helicopter ever developed on the African continent. The full order of 12 aircraft was completed by 2004 at a total development and production cost of R6.2 billion.

ARMSCOR CSH-2 Rooivalk General Arrangement. Three-view technical drawing. Atlas Aircraft Corporation, 15 October 1991

Performance

A Helicopter Built for Africa

Powered by two Turbomeca Makila 1K2 turboshaft engines producing 1,845 shaft horsepower, the Rooivalk was designed for the extreme conditions of the southern African battlespace: temperatures from -35°C to +50°C, operational altitudes up to 20,000 feet, and the dust, humidity, and heat that ground European and Soviet designs.

At its typical mission weight of 7,500 kg, the Rooivalk cruises at 278 km/h with a top speed of 309 km/h. It can fly sideways at 92 km/h. Its hover ceiling of 5,029 metres exceeds the AH-64 Apache (3,866 m), the Mi-28 Havoc (3,600 m), the Ka-50 Kamov (3,600 m), and the Eurocopter Tiger (3,200 m). With external fuel tanks, it can self-deploy over 1,260 km with an endurance exceeding six hours.

The 52 per cent excess hover power gives the Rooivalk a combat agility that allows it to engage targets and disappear behind cover in seconds. Carbon fibre fairings shield the rotor mast from radar. Engine exhaust is directed upward through infrared suppressors into the rotor downwash, dispersing heat to defeat IR-seeking missiles. The main rotor tip speed is kept deliberately low to reduce acoustic signature.

Weapons

The Arsenal

TC-20 hydraulically driven mount with GI2 20mm cannon (left) and APCT ammunition feed belt (right). Source: Denel

The Mk1 carries a GI2 20mm dual-feed cannon under the chin, capable of 740 rounds per minute with 700 rounds of mixed HE-Incendiary and Armour Piercing ammunition. The auto-feed mechanism allows instant switching between ammunition types with the flip of a switch. The cannon traverses 110 degrees in azimuth and can be slaved to the crew's helmet-mounted sight for immediate engagement, with a reaction time of 1.8 seconds from selection to firing at 60 degrees traverse.

The ZT series of ATGM missiles: ZT-3 Swift (bottom), ZT-35 Ingwe with active proximity fuse (middle) and contact probe fuse (top). Source: Paratus Magazine

The original ZT-3 Swift and ZT-35 Ingwe anti-tank missiles proved too slow for airborne deployment, exposing the helicopter to enemy air defence for over 25 seconds. Studies showed that exposure beyond 10 seconds dramatically decreased survivability. The answer was the ZT-6 Mokopa (Black Mamba).

The ZT-6 Mokopa (Black Mamba) precision-guided missile on display. Denel branding and Umkhonto-IR system visible in background. Source: Denel

Designed by Denel Dynamics from 1996, the Mokopa is a 178mm semi-active laser-guided missile with a 10 km range and a circular error probable of just 30 centimetres. Its tandem HEAT warhead penetrates 1,350mm of rolled homogeneous armour and can defeat explosive reactive armour. The missile approaches from above, striking where tanks are least protected. It can be fired in rapid salvo mode, eight seconds apart, with each missile guided to a separate target by different laser designators.

ZT-6 Mokopa missiles on the Rooivalk's stub-wing pylon, ready for deployment. Source: Denel

For air-to-air defence, four Matra Mistral heat-seeking missiles are carried on ATAM launcher pods, with a maximum speed of 2,600 km/h and a 6.5 km range. The complete loadout: 700 cannon rounds, 38 rockets, 8 Mokopa ATGMs, and 4 Mistral air-to-air missiles.

Avionics

Glass Cockpit Pioneer

Weapons Systems Officer cockpit layout with LCD multi-function displays, mission control keyboard, and main sight control grips. Courtesy: Rooivalk, a Legend in the Making

The Rooivalk ADM was the first attack helicopter in the world to fly with a multi-function display glass cockpit. The fully digital avionics system uses the MIL-STD-1553B databus and incorporates night vision goggle-compatible displays, GPS/INS navigation, a FLIR-equipped gyro-stabilised nose turret with auto-tracking (the NightOwl system), and the Thales TopOwl helmet-mounted sight display with integrated Generation IV image intensification.

HMSD symbol legend projected onto the crew member's visor: heading, airspeed, altitude, weapon status, and helmet line-of-sight tracking. Courtesy: Rooivalk, a Legend in the Making

The fire control system allows the crew to pop up from behind cover, scan targets via the recording function, drop back into concealment, identify and prioritise threats on video playback, then re-emerge to attack with pre-designated weapons. Target data can be relayed to other Rooivalks or ground forces via encrypted secure data link. Both pilot and WSO can cue all weapons via their helmets. The pilot can suppress with cannon and rockets while the WSO independently guides Mokopa missiles to armoured targets.

Combat Proven

The DRC: First Blood

The Rooivalk's first combat engagement came on 4 November 2013 against M23 rebels near Chanzu in the eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo, operating as part of the UN Force Intervention Brigade. The white-painted gunships fired 55 rockets against rebel positions while Congolese ground forces advanced. The physical and psychological impact was such that M23 declared an end to their 20-month rebellion the following day.

In December 2015, Rooivalks engaged Islamist Allied Democratic Forces positions near Goma. Ukrainian Mi-25 Hind attack helicopters had been hampered by bad weather. The Rooivalk operated unimpeded, delivering accurate rocket and cannon fire through conditions that grounded Soviet-designed machines. Between 2013 and 2016, three deployed Rooivalks fired over 1,200 rockets and 11,000 cannon rounds in combat. International defence analysts praised the aircraft's all-weather reliability and combat performance.

"Rooivalk is a highly sophisticated digital aircraft. The investment in digital-based systems provides a weapon system capable of achieving ultra-high assurance levels of mission accomplishment in a most demanding operational environment."
Robert Paul Jonkers, Programme Manager for the Rooivalk (1999-2004)

This is the machine that MTN allegedly offered to trade to the Islamic Republic of Iran for a telephone licence. An aircraft conceived under arms embargo by Afrikaner engineers who refused to accept that isolation meant incapacity. A weapons system that took 27 years from first concept to full operational capability. A platform whose combat performance in the DRC proved that the engineering was never the problem. The only question was always what would be done with it, and by whom.
